coredata.dc:title: One-step uniformly hybrid carbon quantum dots with high-reactive TiO2 for photocatalytic application
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wei
Ni, Yaru
Xu, Zhongzi
coredata.dc:description: High-reactive facets dominated anatase TiO2 is of great significance to solve environment and energy challenges. Maintaining the pristine structure and improving the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 still need innovation work by employing different modification processes. Here, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were employed to modify TiO2 with exposed {001}, {101}, and {010} facets by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Results indicate that CQDs can disperse uniformly on TiO2 surface, and the high-reactive facets are maintained perfectly. The introduced CQDs can both enhance the light absorption and suppress photogenerated electron–hole’s recombination. Proper amount of introduced CQDs can both significantly enhance the photocatalytic activities, which are very stable, under the UV and visible light irradiation Corresponding photocatalytic mechanisms are also discussed in the present work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
TiO2
High-reactive facet
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs modified TiO2 photocatalysts

Photoactivity and hydroxide radical (
OH) analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Startup Foundation for Introducing Talent of NUIST

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Startup Foundation for Introducing Talent of NUIST.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of {001}, {101}, and {010} facets co-existed (a and b) pure TiO2 and (c) TiO2–CQDs(12). Inserts in (b) and (c) are the structure models of TiO2 and TiO2–CQDs(12), the yellow dots represent the CQDs. (d) Typical TEM image of a single CQD on the TiO2–CQDs(12) surface. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XRD patterns of TiO2 and TiO2–CQDs(12).
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (b) Raman spectra of as-prepared TiO2 and TiO2–CQDs(12).

(a) Photocatalytic activities of TiO2 and TiO2–CQDs(12) under the UV irradiation; (b) PL spectra of TiO2–CQDs(12) conducted in the terephthalic acid and (b) its intensities compared to that of TiO2 within the same period under the UV irradiation.
(a) Photocatalytic activities of TiO2 and TiO2–CQDs(12), and (b) repeated photocatalytic activity measurement (four cycles) of TiO2–CQDs(12) in decomposing RhB under the visible light irradiation.
Effect of different CQDs amounts on the photocatalytic activities of TiO2–CQDs(x) (x =4, 8, 12, 20) under the visible light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the important roles of CQDs for enhancing the photocatalytic activity of {001}, {101}, and {010} co-existed TiO2 under the UV and visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: One-step uniformly hybrid carbon quantum dots with high-reactive TiO2 for photocatalytic application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology, Nanjing 210044, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering
Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 210009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-reactive facets dominated anatase TiO2 is of great significance to solve environment and energy challenges. Maintaining the pristine structure and improving the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 still need innovation work by employing different modification processes. Here, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were employed to modify TiO2 with exposed {001}, {101}, and {010} facets by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Results indicate that CQDs can disperse uniformly on TiO2 surface, and the high-reactive facets are maintained perfectly. The introduced CQDs can both enhance the light absorption and suppress photogenerated electron–hole’s recombination. Proper amount of introduced CQDs can both significantly enhance the photocatalytic activities, which are very stable, under the UV and visible light irradiation Corresponding photocatalytic mechanisms are also discussed in the present work.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysts (TiO2, ZnO, and BiVO4 etc.) exposed with different high-reactive facets have been studied widely all over the world [1–3]. Employing these specific structures can promote the process of utilizing solar energy for environment protection and new energy generation [4–6]. To data, TiO2 is considered as one of the most promising photocatalysts for large scale industrial application. However, according to the Wulff structure of anatase TiO2, more than ca. 96% of the surface was covered by the {001} facet which possesses the highest thermodynamicstability [2,7,8]. By employing acid environment and F as the controlling agent, recent investigations have proven that introduce high-energetic facets ({110} (1.09J/m2)>{001} (0.90J/m2)>{010} (0.53J/m2)>{101} (0.44J/m2)) to replace the {101} facet can significantly enhance the photocatalytic activity [1,9–11]. However, even various reaction systems have been discovered to synthesize such kinds of TiO2, the wide band gap (ca. 3.2eV) and relatively fast recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes are still the drawbacks in inhibiting the TiO2 photocatalytic activity and application.

In order to enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2, various methods, such as (non)metal doping, noble metal deposition, and coupling with semiconductors, have been applied [12–17]. Besides these efficient methods, introducing different kinds of quantum dots are also widely investigated [18,19]. Recently, CQDs have become a star in this area for their low-toxicity and abundant physical–chemical properties compared to traditional metal-based quantum dots [20–23]. CQDs can disperse well in water and are suitable to modify various organic and inorganic materials. Notably, CQDs also possess excellent upconversion photoluminescence property, which is depended on the excitation wavelength and dot size [20,24,25]. These excellent properties made CQDs a kind of very promising material for photocatalysis, solar cells, biomaterials etc. application [24,26,27]. On the other hand, quantum dots can serve to trap and transfer the electrons, which is the key factor in enhancing the photocatalytic activity.

Considering the excellent properties of CQDs and TiO2 with exposed high-reactive facets, the combination of both the materials may be a good system for efficient photocatalysis. Moreover, this system can also be used to study whether the introduced CQDs have any negative effect on the property of the existed high-reactive facets. Herein, we report the one-pot hydrothermal method to fabricate CQDs modified high-reactive TiO2 exposed with the {001}, {101}, and {010} facets on a Ti foil. By this method, the CQDs can deposit onto the TiO2 surface uniformly. It is found that the CQDs can significantly enhance the light utilization and separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes. Thus both the photocatalytic activities under the UV and visible light irradiation can be enhanced significantly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs modified TiO2 photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: All Chemicals were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. In a typical experiment for growing TiO2 on the Ti foil: a piece of Ti foil (purity: 99.7%, size: 20∗20∗0.25mm) was washed by cyclohexanone and ethanol, and then dried naturally. Afterwards, the cleaned Ti foil was put into a 60ml Teflon-lined autoclave containing 40ml HF aqueous solution (concentration: 0.08M). After heated at 200°C for 2h, the obtained white film sample, named TiO2, was washed with deionized water and dried at 60°C [28].

In order to simply deposit CQDs on TiO2, 12mg of sodiumcitrate was dissolved in the HF aqueous solution to form a homogeneous solution [29]. The rest procedures were the same with above. The obtained sample with dark color was named TiO2–CQDs(12). 4mg, 8mg, and 20mg of sodium citrate was also dissolve into the initial reaction solution to adjust amount of introduced CQDs, the obtained samples were named TiO2–CQDs(4), TiO2–CQDs(8), and TiO2–CQDs(20).

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements of as-prepared samples were conducted on an ARL X’TRA X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.15406nm) at a scanning rate of 5°/min. Raman analysis was conducted on a Labram HR800 spectrometer with 514nm laser as the excitation source under ambient conditions. UV–Vis light absorption spectrum was measured by a Shimadzu 3101 spectrophotometer with BaSO4 as the reference sample. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed with an S-4800 scanning electron analyzer. Transmittance electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was conducted on a JEM-2010 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Photoactivity and hydroxide radical (
OH) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical process, a piece of the photocatalytic film was placed at the bottom of a cylindrical quartz culture dish, which was surrounded by the circulatingcoolingwater, containing Rhodamine B (RhB) aqueous solution (15ml, 20mg/L). In order to guarantee the photocatalyst can be excited by the irradiated light, the solution depth was ca. 3mm. The culture dish was then placed under the irradiation of a 14W UV lamp or a 500W xenon lamp fixed with an UV filter to obtain visible light (λ >420nm). Prior to the photocatalytic reaction, the solution was kept in the dark with magnetic stirring for 60min to establish the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. During the measurement process, 2ml of the solution was collected at certain time intervals to measure the RhB absorption spectrum (peak center: 553nm).

The generated
OH was analyzed by the PL technique by employing terephthalic acid as the probe molecule [30]. Highly fluorescent compound, 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid, can be formed when terephthalic acid interacted with the produced
OH and the PL intensity was in proportion to the total amount of
OH generated in the water solution. The analysis procedure was almost the same with the photocatalytic activity measurement except the RhB aqueous solution was replaced by the terephthalic acid aqueous solution (1mmol/L) with a concentration of NaOH (4mmol/L). The PL emission spectra of 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid were recorded on the FL3-221 fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature, and the excitation and emission wavelength were 315nm and 425nm, respectively.

TiO2 particles exposed with the {001}, {101}, and {010} facets has been prepared successfully by several researchers, and the typical crystal structure is also well-known [9,31]. In the present study, SEM images of as-prepared TiO2 is shown in Fig. 1 (a) and (b). Compared to TiO2 particles exposed only with the {001} and {101} facets, Fig. 1(a) reveals that the obtained cross-linked particles have an additional quartet column at the center. The angle of ca. 112° shown in Fig. 1(b) between the lateral trapezoid and the top square of a single particle is accord with the angle between the {001} and {101} facets. According to previous studies and Wulff structure, the obtained TiO2 (corresponding structure model is inserted in Fig. 1(b)) can be identified exposed with the {001}, {101}, and {010} facets. Fig. 1(c) shows the SEM image of TiO2–CQDs(12), it can be observed that its surface become rough compared to that of pure TiO2. Moreover, all the CQDs are uniformly deposited on TiO2 surface, indicating the CQDs deposition has no selectivity (Fig. 1(c), inset). To confirm the presence of CQDs, the TEM image shown in Fig. 1(d) reveals the surface deposited nanoparticle (CQDs size: ca. 10nm) has an interplanar spacing of ca. 0.32nm, corresponding to the (002) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon [20]. This strongly evidenced the formation of CQDs modified TiO2 photocatalyst.

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of as-prepared TiO2 and TiO2–CQDs(12), which is in good agreement with the standard data for anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21-1272). No extra characteristic peaks of Ti foil are detected, indicating that the TiO2 film is thick enough to resist the X-ray coming to the Ti foil. The characteristic peak for CQDs at ca. 26° is also not observed, which may because of CQDs are uniformly dispersed and its relatively low diffraction intensity compared to that of TiO2.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of the as-prepared TiO2 and TiO2–CQDs(12) are shown in Fig. 3 (a). It is clear that both the samples have the same absorption edge of ca. 385nm, which is the characteristic property of anatase TiO2 [32]. In addition, because of the presence of CQDs, TiO2–CQDs(12) has a higher light absorption in the range of 385–700nm. This phenomenon is ascribed to the intrinsic property of CQDs which may help to improve the photocatalytic activity under the visible light irradiation. In order to investigate whether the introduced CQDs affect the structure of TiO2, Raman analysis of TiO2 and TiO2–CQDs(12) is conducted (Fig. 3(b)). We can see that both the samples have the characteristic peaks of anatase TiO2 (Eg : 144cm−1, B 1
g : 393cm−1, A 1
g : 510cm−1, and Eg : 629cm−1) [33]. The peak position and their relatively intensity are the same, indicating TiO2 and TiO2–CQDs have almost the same structured TiO2. Moreover, two more characteristic peaks located at about 1333 and 1600cm−1 are observed in TiO2–CQDs(12), which can be ascribed to the D-band and G-band of carbon, respectively [34,35]. The D-band is resulted from the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the plane terminations of disordered graphite, while the G-band is resulted from the ordered graphite structure. All these results may allow us to draw the conclusion that CQDs are simply deposited on the TiO2–CQDs(12) surface, and have none negative effects on the structure properties of TiO2 by the exposure of {001}, {101}, and {010} facets.

As is known, TiO2 is an efficient UV-driven photocatalyst. Photocatalytic activities of TiO2 and TiO2–CQDs(12) under the UV irradiation were measured by employing RhB as the degradation target to study the effect of introduced CQDs. Fig. 4 (a) shows that TiO2 decomposed about 76% of the RhB dye in 75min, while TiO2–CQDs(12) almost decomposed all the RhB dye within the same period. Based on the results, we can draw the conclusion that CQDs play a positive effect on enhancing the UV photocatalytic activity of TiO2. To further illustrate the results,
OH generated in the photocatalytic systems were analyzed. Fig. 4(b) reveals that the amount of generated
OH in TiO2–CQDs(12) photocatalytic reaction system increased gradually along with the irradiation time, indicating TiO2–CQDs(12) can be excited persistently and the carriers can transfer to the surface for photocatalysis. More importantly, the amount of the
OH is much more than that generated in the TiO2 photocatalytic reaction system within the same period. More photogenerated electrons and holes are separated in TiO2–CQDs(12), more high-reactive radicles will be formed for photocatalytic reactions.

Based on our experiment results, we will explain the possible mechanism of CQDs in improving the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 exposed with the {001}, {101}, and {010} facets. {001} and {010} facets can truly give higher photocatalytic activity than the traditional {101} facets of TiO2. But this improvement is still not enough to extend TiO2 application. When CQDs was deposited onto TiO2, it will play two important roles during the photocatalytic process (Fig. 7 ). Firstly, CQDs can serve as photogenerated electron collectors and transporters, thus the electrons and holes transferred to the TiO2 surface can be separated more efficiently. Then the electrons and holes will have more time to serve as reaction centers or to form other high-reactive radicles (such as
OH, and
O2−) to decompose pollutants [24]. Secondly, CQDs can help to absorb more light for exciting TiO2, which is somewhat similar to the effect of graphene and carbon nanotube. Based on previous reports, CQDs also possess upconversion properties. They will absorb longer wavelength light and emit shorter wavelength light in the UV and visible light range [37]. These high-energetic photons will serve to excite TiO2 for photocatalysis more easily and efficiently. As a result, the CQDs modified TiO2 can use a broad range of the sunlight. More importantly, the total improvement of the photocatalytic activity may indicate the physically introduced CQDs do not have any negative effects on the TiO2 active facets (such as {001} and {010}). CQDs just serve as a sensitizer or additive may open a new way to further improve the photocatalytic properties of semiconductor photocatalysts with special structures.

In summary, anatase TiO2 exposed with the {001}, {101}, and {010} facets has been grown on the Ti foil. Further introduction of mono-dispersed CQDs onto TiO2 whole surface can significantly improve both the UV-driven and visible-light-driven photocatalytic activities. The CQDs almost have no negative effect on the TiO2 active facets, and can help to improve the photogenerated electron–hole separation efficiency and absorb longer wavelength light for photocatalytic reaction. Optimizing the proportion of CQDs to TiO2 is also very important in the present study. Our work may encourage new ideas on the design of high-efficient photocatalysts with additives and special crystal structures to solve environmental and energy problems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Startup Foundation for Introducing Talent of NUIST.
